title: Anxiety: Levels and Management
subtitle: The nurse cares for an anxious patient — recognizing mild, moderate, severe, and panic levels, staying with the patient, using a calm voice and simple directions, and teaching coping skills.

patient.name: Anxiety
patient.admitDate: The nurse supports a patient experiencing anxiety.

nursesNote: The nurse supports a patient experiencing anxiety, a vague feeling of dread that activates the body's stress response. Anxiety occurs on a continuum. Mild anxiety sharpens awareness and is the best time to teach, since the patient is alert and learns well. Moderate anxiety narrows the perceptual field, so the patient needs help focusing and benefits from redirection. Severe anxiety leaves the patient unable to focus or solve problems, so teaching is deferred. Panic is a loss of control with possible disorganized behavior or fleeing, making safety the priority. The nurse stays with an anxious patient rather than leaving, uses a calm, low voice with short and simple directions, and reduces stimulation. As anxiety eases, the nurse guides slow, deep breathing and teaches coping and grounding skills, helping the patient name the feeling and identify triggers. A PRN anxiolytic may be given for severe or panic-level anxiety if ordered. The nurse avoids false reassurance and instead acknowledges the feeling and provides steady, supportive presence.
providerOrders: Assess the patient's level of anxiety (mild, moderate, severe, panic)
Stay with the patient and provide a calm, low-stimulation environment
Use a calm voice with short, simple directions
Teach slow breathing and coping skills as anxiety eases
Give a PRN anxiolytic for severe or panic-level anxiety if ordered

opts.text: Mild anxiety is the best time to teach
Moderate anxiety narrows the patient's focus
Severe anxiety makes it hard to focus or learn
Panic is a calm, controlled state
Anxiety is a feeling of dread with a stress response
The nurse should leave a panicked patient alone

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What guides care of the anxious patient?

opts.text: A calm, low voice with simple words helps
Reducing stimulation lowers anxiety
Detailed teaching works well during panic
Slow, deep breathing lowers arousal
Safety is the priority during panic
False reassurance is the best response

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order helping a patient with rising anxiety

opts.text: Stay with the patient and ensure safety
Reduce stimulation and use a calm voice
Guide slow, deep breathing
Help the patient name the feeling and triggers
Teach coping skills once calmer
Alert, focused, and ready to learn
Unable to focus or follow directions
Focus narrows but responds to cues
Loss of control with disorganized behavior
Slightly tense but problem-solving well
Feels a sense of doom and may try to flee

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient is pacing, hyperventilating, and cannot focus
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stay with the patient in a safe, quiet area
Speak calmly with short, simple directions
Coach slow breathing together
Give a PRN anxiolytic if ordered
Reassess and document the response
Staying with the patient during panic
Saying 'Just calm down, there's nothing to worry about'
Using a calm voice and short directions
Starting detailed discharge teaching during panic
Coaching slow, deep breathing
Leaving the severely anxious patient alone
Reducing noise and bright stimulation
Teaching coping skills once the patient is calmer
